turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Number 770, Ted Steven Chimel, petitioner versus California. Mr. Monroe.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court. The Court considers today a case which, I believe goes to the very core of the Fourth Amendment of the Constitution. The facts of the case, in February 1965, there was a burglary of the Pulati home in Santa Ana, California. Number of coins were taken, some metals and tokens, containers and so forth.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: This wasn't currency. These were rare coins or --

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Rare coins.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Collector's items.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Collector's items, Your Honor, yes. Thereafter, in August of 1965, there was a burglary of a coin shop, the Money Vault. This took place in Orange, California nearby. Without going into a great deal of detail, there was suspicion of the petitioner as to the Pulati burglary but I think it was pretty much agreed and appears in the record, that way, that there was no probable cause on which his arrest could have been founded.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: He was a fellow member of a collector's club with the Pulati's, was he?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: And he missed a meeting one night and that's the night at the Pulati's house was burglarized?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. Also prior to that time, he had talked to them about insurance whether or not the coins were insured. He knew, of course, as a fellow member of coin clubs that they were coin collectors and that they did have coins in their home. As to the Money Vault burglary, the petitioner made a statement before the burglary to a neighbor that he had a big deal cooking that night and that he was going to knock over. I don't know whether he said coin shop exactly. He was going to knock over a place, anyhow. Thereafter, to the same neighbor he made a statement the day following the burglary that an attempt had been made to enter, which had been unsuccessful. And some two weeks later he made another statement to another coin shop operator that he had been involved in the Money Vault matter. Now, then, this was about the middle of August. On August 25th, the California Supreme Court found that petitioner was arrested without a warrant, on the street, in Santa Ana, California, by the investigating officer here, Officer Del Coma. After transportation to the Orange Police Station and some interrogation, petitioner was released. Then on September 13th, something more than two weeks later, Officer Del Coma went to the appropriate magistrate and submitted a complaint in purely conclusionary form. The complaint is set out in the petitioner's brief. On the basis of this complaint which, in substance, merely said, “I believe that this man committed a burglary on such and such a date in this county”; on the basis of this complaint, a warrant of arrest issued at 10:39 a.m. on September 13th. Officer Del Coma doesn't give us too clear a picture of what he did the rest of that day. He knew where the petitioner was at that time. He knew where he worked. He knew where to go and get him. But in any case, Officer Del Coma arrived at the petitioner's home something shortly after 4:00 p.m., knowing at that time that he was not home. He gained admittance to the house, waited for him to come home.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Was there somebody else in the house?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: His wife was there, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Does it show how he gained entry into the house? Did he show the wife the arrest warrant, or doesn't the record show?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: The record simply shows that the officer requested admittance to wait for Mr. Chimel, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, is that to say that at least his presence, the officer's presence is not an unlawful presence inside the house, based on the consent of the wife?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: I don't think this record is clear enough to show one way or the other, Your Honor. We don't contend that the officer's presence was unlawful at that point. Mr. Chimel came home at something like 4:30 p.m., whereupon the officer, after he had entered the house, placed him under arrest on the authority of the warrant, showed him the warrant, requested permission to search, which was not granted. The officer then stated that, having executed a lawful arrest on a felony warrant, he had a right to search, and he was going to, and proceeded to do so. This search covered every room in the house. It covered the garage. It covered an inspection in the attic and it involved the opening of drawers, the opening of boxes, cupboards. It was described by the officer as a general search. The officer did not have knowledge, at the time he made this search of any -- of information which led him to believe that any specific property was to be found in that house.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: What's your position about the arrest? Was that a lawful arrest on that warrant?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: I can't answer this simply yes or no, Your Honor, for the reason that in this case we raised the issue, so far as I know for the first time in California, that the arrest warrant had been unlawfully issued.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Under State law?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Under Federal law, Giordenello versus U.S. and Aguilar that --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: The California Court so held, didn't it?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: The California court did so hold, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: But then -- so we now have a ruling of the State Courts that this warrant is invalid.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Only -- Didn't they rule that it was invalid under California law?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: No, Your Honor. It was under Giordenello, Aguilar, and Barnes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Cases from this Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: The arrest warrant itself, on a probable cause basis.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. This brings us then to the alternative holding of the California Court that it was a valid probable cause arrest. If I may leave that point for a moment, it ties in with a later part of the argument.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, I suppose it's going to be relevant, isn't it to what we have to decide as to the scope of the search, whether that arrest was or was not lawful, whether on the basis of the warrant or on the basis of probable cause in respect to the law?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. I am a little bit on the horns of a dilemma in that. I don't want to concede the arrest was lawful, because I have doubts. I think that the search was bad for other reasons. I simply don't want to make the concession.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And well, without making a concession, I guess you are going to argue on the assumption that it was lawful, nevertheless in scope, this exceeded the speed limit under the Fourth Amendment.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: This is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But you are going to argue, aren't you, that there should be no arrest without a warrant at all?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I don't intend to make that argument.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: You do concede that you may arrest without a warrant, constitutionally?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Oh! Certainly, I am sure that there are myriad of cases where an arrest without warrant should be valid.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Oh! I agree with that. I just wondered what your contention was.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, I don't understand your position in view of what you said. I thought that you were arguing to this Court that the arrest was an unlawful arrest, in these particular circumstances. And that, second, even if the arrest was lawful, which you say it was not, the search was of an impermissible scope. Now, is that your position or is it not? That is what I thought was indicated by your petition for cert, and by your brief here?

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: My primary focus, Your Honor, is on the search itself. I also take a second position that on the facts of this case, a valid warrant should have been used. However, my primary focus is on the search itself.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I understand that, but I am trying to find out, following my brother Brennan's question to you, whether secondarily, or however -- whatever order you want to do it, you challenge the lawfulness of the arrest here.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Yes, that's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I thought you did, but if you don't, I'd certainly like to know. As I read your papers, you certainly challenged the lawfulness of the arrest here on the theory that, as the California Courts held, the warrant was invalid, that without the warrant the arrest here was unlawful and in violation of the Fourth Amendment.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: If I can clarify, Your Honor, to back up and change the facts a little bit, if the officer had simply put the petitioner under arrest at his home, and taken him down to the station, had that been all that had gone on, I would not challenge that arrest on probable cause.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: And you would not challenge that arrest on the grounds that he should have obtained a warrant?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Because you say there was. Is it your position that whenever the police have probable cause to arrest, regardless of the time factor or any other circumstances, they don't have to get a warrant? Is that your position?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That is not my position, Your Honor, but so far as the arrest which involves no search, I don't think that it is fairly before this Court on this record.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I don't understand that at all.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, you would have a hard time getting a remedy if that is all you had, as far as this case is concerned. You might have a civil action or something, but you would have a hard time arguing that an otherwise valid conviction had to fall because the arrest was an improper one. We have never held anything like that, have we?

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: No, but involved in this, as I understand it, one of the arguments that the State makes is that the search and the seizure were lawful as incident to a valid arrest.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Now, do you or do you not argue that the arrest was invalid as one branch of your submission to this Court?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: For the sake of argument, Your Honor, I'll concede that this arrest was invalid, for the sake of argument here.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: No, no. I'm not asking you to concede anything. I'm trying to find out what your position is. I have read your petition and your brief here, and you surprised me by what you said. But if that's your position, then that's your position.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Pursuing the facts of the case, then, after this search, or going back to this search of September 13. It was based altogether on a charge of burglary of the Money Vault. So far as the record shows, the only thing which was missing from the Money Vault was coins, nothing else. Nevertheless, in this search, a large quantity of coins and medals, tokens, boxes, razor blades, containers, various other non-coin items were seized.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Were these the -- were these the fruit of another burglary?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: This was what later developed, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Did the police know it at the time?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Didn't they -- they never even knew of the other robbery or burglary?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: The record indicates that Officer Del Coma, who is the central figure here, had heard something of the Pulati burglary, the first burglary, but that --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: He doesn't know what was stolen there?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: I think he knew there were coins which had been taken, but he did not have any detailed information so far as the record shows.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Assume if he had known specifically what was stolen in the Pulati case and he encountered it in the house that he searched, in the course of searching for coins stolen in another burglary, would you say he couldn't seize the fruit of the Pulati?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Oh, no. If, Your Honor, he were in the course of a legal search, I think under Marron he would have a right to seize contraband or stolen goods which he happened to come across in the course of a legal search, although I don't think this was a legal search at all here.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: So this is -- this is your fundamental question, the legality of the search?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Without either a valid arrest warrant or without a valid search warrant?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, the legality of the search, as I understand your case, depends upon two issues. First of all, the search, I suppose one that would argue, was obviously invalid if the underlying arrest was invalid.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Because the only justification for the search is that it was incident to a lawful arrest. Therefore, if the arrest was not lawful, the search could not be lawful, regardless of it scope. And your second point is, even if the arrest was a lawful arrest, this search which is justified only as an incident to a lawful arrest was far too wide in scope to be incident to a lawful arrest. Is that -- is that your submission?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor, plus on additional point. I suggest that in order to make a search as an incident to an arrest, there should be a showing of the same elements of probable cause thereafter which would have justified issuance of a search warrant in the first place, else, you are proceeding with a search which is not justified by as much showing, you are proceeding without a warrant with less showing than you would be had you proceeded with a warrant, the preferred method of procedure.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, there can certainly -- let's assume a lawful street arrest of somebody that you have reasonable cause to believe has a concealed weapon and let's assume the arrest, that there is probable cause, sufficient probable cause, and that you do stop and arrest that man, then certainly incident to that arrest you can search him. You would agree with that?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: No quarrel.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: And if in addition or if instead of a gun, you don't find a gun at all, but what you do find is narcotics on him, right in his pocket. Although there was never any ground for a search warrant to search his pockets for narcotics, would you agree or not that that's a perfectly valid search and seizure of the narcotics.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Oh! Certainly Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Even though there was no ground at all to search him for narcotics.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Searches of the person, I think, stand on an altogether different plane, because the officer arresting a person obviously has a primary need to search that man and see that he is not assaulted with some sort of weapon. I don't think there's any question there. There's an obvious necessity.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, let's see, then, Mr. Monroe. This gets down to except for searches of the persons -- except for searches of the person. There has to be both probable cause to arrest and independent of the arrest, probable cause to search.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: If I may, Your Honor, I wouldn't say really that it's independent of the probable cause to arrest. Rather, turning to --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, in addition to, rather than independent.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: There has to be that other element of some reason to think, if I, as the officer, am going to search the place of arrest, then I have to have some reason to think --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, all of our cases so far, I gather, have sustained searches, so long as there was probable cause to make the arrest and that the search was incident to that arrest, whether or not the officers had knowledge of what it was they might pick up. That be so?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: I think there's a strong suggestion in the cases, Your Honor. The cases which have sustained searches that this Court in fact has over the years sustained those searches which have proceeded in this context with a valid arrest warrant and a very strong suggestion that the officer did have knowledge that, what I want at least has a likelihood of being there. It becomes almost impossible to explain the exploratory search cases unless there is something of that nature.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well what if -- well, what more do you need -- what more do you suggest we need if you have probable cause, or a warrant to arrest a fellow for stealing some coins, and you go to his house and arrest him in his house? Now you suggest you need more reason to search his house for the coins than the fact that you think he stole them?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: You have to have some reason to believe that he has not only stolen them but that he has actually got them there?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And you suggest our cases support that submission?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Has that ever -- has that question ever been explicitly addressed in any of our opinions?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: No. The cases in this Court --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: You get more comfort out of the pre-Harris cases than you do out of the Harris.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: The Harris case is not comforting at all, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Or Rabinowitz.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: No. Rabinowitz, I think, is squarely an issue, so far as its language, the warrant is not required.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Really, the essence of your position is that you want us to re-examine Rabinowitz and Harris, and that is a perfectly understandable position.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: That's the guts of your case, isn't it?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: You think your re-examination would go so far as overruling, wouldn't it?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: I don't think it would be necessary. Let me clarify my position here, too, because, of course, when one suggests a re-examination of Rabinowitz, the mind automatically turns to the revival of Trupiano. I'm not really suggesting this, because I think Trupiano was a very difficult case on its own facts. Trupiano, it may be recalled, involved a valid arrest of Antonio, who was then committing a felony, and the still, the paraphernalia for committing the felony was in plain view. I think possibly Trupiano went too far in not permitting the still to come into evidence. There was, after all, a valid probable cause arrest, and this still was seen in the course of making that arrest. Trupiano raised some, I think, very definite problems there. In terms of results, the cases in this Court which have generally been supported -- it might be noted here, too, that Rabinowitz so often cited as a non-warrant case, was a case, in fact, in which there was a valid arrest warrant and there was every reason to think the very property for which the search was made was in the place which was searched. Every element which would have been necessary to get a search warrant was known to the officer and was almost unquestionably present. The other cases which have supported similar searches are United States versus Ventresca, Rugendorf, Jones versus the United States 362 U.S., Abel versus U.S. 362, of course Rabinowitz which was with a warrant, U.S. versus Lefkowitz, and Marron versus U.S.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I take it, then, that your position certainly would be that if the officer went to the magistrate and said, “I have probable cause to believe that so-and-so stole some coins,” and if the magistrate agreed with him that he had probable cause he nevertheless couldn't automatically also get a search warrant?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: I don't --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: You couldn't say to the magistrate, “Well, you agree with me that I have probable cause to believe that John Jones stole these coins, and you are giving me the arrest warrant for his arrest; therefore, give me a search warrant for his house.” You would say he would have to have more than that to get a search warrant?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Certainly, Your Honor, because he would have to particularly describe for the magistrate the place to be searched and the persons or things to be seized.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, it describes the house.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: But the person or things to be seized.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: He also says the coins may be there.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: He says the coins that were stolen here, the description of the coins.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: But I think under the Camara, he would have to give some reason for thinking that they were there.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, because the man stole them, and that's where he lives.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: If this were before a magistrate, and the magistrate passed on it and felt that there was a sufficient showing, taking all of the facts in such and such --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, but would you agree there was a sufficient showing? If there was -- if you agreed there was probable cause to arrest and you agreed that the arrest warrant, validly issued, would you agree there was then probable cause to issue the search warrant to search his house where he lived?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: The cause shown is merely enough to arrest, if that is the cause shown, I do not think that is enough to search his house.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: What more must he show?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Some reason for thinking that --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: That he stashed the coins there instead of someplace else.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, suppose the officer says, “I would assume that he has them in his home.”

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: If the officer assumed that they were in his possession?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: If he told the magistrate that, “One thing I am sure, he did rob this place. I am certain of that, and I assume that the coins will be in his home.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: On the -- let me turn to the facts in this case --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Isn't it your position that he has to so more than assume that they are there?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Oh! Certainly.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Isn't that what your position is?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: There has to be something other than an unfounded assumption, certainly.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: That the coins are there; like somebody saw him put them there?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Or he had conducted himself in such a manner as to support belief that they are there.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: How far, going back to the second point, when he's arrested, how far do you go beyond search of a person? The room?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: I think there can be no question, Your Honor, about the validity -- let me separate, first of all, searches and seizures. Many of the cases treat them as one and the same. I think there can be no question about seizure of evidence, instrumentalities, contraband, in plain view. Searches of the person, of course, are conceded. As far as searches beyond that, it is very, very hard, I think, to find a logical stopping point, because we start with Rabinowitz, which permitted a very narrowly circumscribed search of one room. Now, we are searching entire houses.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You'd say -- you would draw one line on the attic, wouldn't you?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Sir?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You'd draw one line in the attic? If you would draw the other line at his person, and then you wouldn't try to draw a line anyplace else? You would say certainly he couldn't search the attic.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Oh! I think, Your Honor, that the only logical line that can be drawn is the person, and those things which are in plain view of the person at the place of arrest. When it involves going into containers, so to speak, then I think the legitimate scope of the search has been exceeded.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well what happens if he arrests him, takes him down, and announces to everybody in the house that he is arresting him for stealing these coins, and then gets a search warrant. Do you think the search warrant would be worth anything?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: It seems to me, Your Honor, in looking at the facts of this case, there were three officers. It would have been a very simple thing, if they wanted to do so, to leave one officer there and obtain a search warrant.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But you say he couldn't get one anyway, if he went after it, because he had absolutely no probable cause to think the coins were in the house.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's true, Your Honor, on the facts of this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: So the officers ought to leave. Their just aren't going to get to search ever.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: The officer describes this as a general search. He wasn't searching for anything specific, so it seems to me clear on his testimony that he was exploring this house.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Just for the fun of it. He wasn't looking for coins as the product of the crime for which he was arresting him?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: I questioned him on that and he didn't so testify.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Isn't this case somewhat confusing because there were two burglaries involving coins, and the police officer went there with probable cause to arrest for one of them.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: He had no knowledge of the other one, and he made a general search of the whole house, and found nothing in connection with the burglary on which they had probably cause, but found some things on the other burglary for which they had no probable cause when they went there, and had no information.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: That is true, Your Honor. This is a hard point here, and I think it bears emphasis that the case on which they had probable cause was coins and coins alone, so far as the record shows, and the seizure was of medals, tokens, boxes, wallets, razor blades, things which weren't even in the same class.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: This is something like the facts in Mapp, where they went looking, I think, for gambling apparatus, or something of that kind, and they made a general search and ended up finding some obscene literature in a trunk in the place. That would be somewhat comparable to this, wouldn't it?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: It is, Your Honor, yes. I see that my time has expired.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes but Mr. Monroe, we have taken all your time by answering questions, and I think we would like to hear something about Rabinowitz and Harris, because that is the thrust of your case, and I suggest that you may have five minutes for rebuttal, if you want, after hearing from the other side, if you would want.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And, of course, you may have five minutes extra if you wish it, too. Mr. George.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: The issues in this case basically are was the search, which was an incident of the concededly valid arrest on probable cause, rendered unreasonable solely by virtue of the fact that it extended beyond the room in which petitioner was arrested? Secondly, was it rendered unreasonable by virtue of the fact that there was a delay of some six hours in executing the warrant of arrest? And thirdly, was it rendered unreasonable solely by virtue of the fact that it was conducted as an incident to an arrest on probable cause, instead of by way of a search warrant?

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: In what basis did the California Court hold that the warrant was invalid?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: The California Supreme Court, in this case and in the companion case of People versus Sesslin, held that the procedure which had been followed in the State of California for many, many years, of issuing warrants upon complaints which did not set out the factual elements of probable cause, were invalid, but the Court held that, although this arrest warrant was invalid, there was ample probable cause --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I understand that. Well do you -- what about the second point that you listed there? Why do we have to consider that, the delay of six hours in executing a warrant?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Is that involved in this case?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That has been raised by the petitioner. We consider it a purely frivolous point that's hardly an unreasonable delay. But petitioner contends that the execution of the arrest was deliberately delayed so as to permit the officers to search the house.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: You mean, assuming that the warrant was otherwise valid, petitioner argues that its effect was spent, or it was invalid or ineffective because of the delay.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Is that the point?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes, but the State Supreme Court specifically upheld the arrest on the theory of probable cause apart from the warrant. And I would submit that one could not find a case on which there was more probable cause to arrest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Did they search the house before Mr. Chimel came back?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: No, they did not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: They waited for him. They went to the house, found he wasn't there, wanted to arrest him, and they waited for him.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: They waited approximately 10 minutes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And they arrested him, and then the search followed that, is that it?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I take it that your point is that the arrest here was valid because there was probable cause, and that the -- and that no warrant was necessary.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That is correct. The officer --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Is there any case that you can think of in which a warrant is necessary and can be lawfully obtained consistent with the Fourth Amendment?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Is your --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: In other words, doesn't this position really read out of existence -- read out of existence -- the need for a warrant?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: No, it does not. First of all, may I ask Your Honor for a clarification? Are you speaking of warrants of arrest when you speak of search warrants?

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I'm talking -- let's take the arrest warrant. First, is there any case where an arrest warrant could be obtained under the Fourth Amendment and would be necessary on the theory in which this case is being argued by you, and I expect by your adversary?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Our position is no and even --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: In other words, police never need a warrant. If they have probable cause, they can get a warrant, of course, for purposes of the Fourth Amendment but they don't need it, because if they have probable cause, they can go ahead and arrest.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: And so all this business of going to a magistrate to get a warrant in the terms of the warrant is wasted time.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: I don't think it's wasted time and the officer here certainly didn't think it was wasted time. He testified expressly that it was his habit that whenever possible --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I know. Well, maybe it's a nice thing for officers to do, but no legal requirement.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That's correct. Even Trupiano said that.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Yes. Now, second, turning to the search warrant, your position is exactly the same, isn't it, that is to say that, wherever there is probable cause to search, there is no need to obtain the warrant?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: No that's not --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: On the other hand, if there's not probable cause, the warrant cannot be obtained.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: You don't say that, do you?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That is not our position, because clearly a search may not be made upon probable cause. A search may be made only as an incident to an arrest upon probable cause, and we would submit that whenever there is a proper arrest, and that the search is number one, reasonable; and number two, incident to the arrest, that then there is no need to obtain a search warrant.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Can you suggest a situation to me in which there is probable cause for arrest and not probable cause to search?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes. One situation which comes readily to mind is the arrest of a person -- let's say Mr. Chimel was arrested at a friend's house. Now, there would be the right to search incident to arrest, but perhaps not the same type of search as you have here, because it would not be predictable that the stolen coins would be at this friend's house, or at a public place.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, what you're really saying is that there may be a difference in the permissible scope of the search, but that whenever there is probable cause to arrest, there is probable cause to conduct some sort of search.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Is that what you are saying?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That is our position.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: How far could they go on the searching in the hypothetical that you just gave us?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That might depend upon who this friend was, whether he had some connection with the burglary or not. Now, under current law, there does not seem to be any definite limitation. This Court has always said that we must view the legality of the particular search in light of its reasonableness under the facts of the case, and there can't be any mechanical standards here. I think what significant is that the Fourth Amendment, as it was drawn up by the framers, does not confer a narrow grant of the power to search. What it does is it restricts the power to search, the existing and recognized power to search, and prohibits only searches that are unreasonable. And in turn, that determination of reasonableness is a very practical concept and it has been since it evolved under common law.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr. Chimel -- may I break in just one moment on the other point? Assuming that they know, they are confident that this man committed this robbery, and they see him in this side of town, going toward his home. Is it possible that some detective would say, “Well, if we arrest him here, we can only search his person; but if we let him go home, and then arrest him, we can search the attic, the garage, and anything else”, is that possible?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: It is possible that officers would do that and definite --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, what is different about that case and this case?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: I think it's improper when the officers do that. We concede that, and there are cases so holding, that it's improper.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, didn't they deliberately wait at home for him? They knew where he was.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: No, they didn't. If I may answer your question by chronicling the events of that day, I would like to point out that the arrest warrant issued at 10:39 in the morning. The officer submitted the arrest warrant which he had obtained and which, under current California law was a valid arrest warrant. Two, the warrant detail of his department, this was customary practice of his department, and this was done as a general rule, without exception, unless there was a definite information that the suspect was about to flee. The Warrant detail processed the warrant until 2 o'clock that afternoon. Now, both prior to 2:00 p.m. and after 2:00 p.m., the officer was involved in other burglary investigations. He had a monthly caseload of 45 to 80 burglaries. He was involved with those burglaries. He was not required; it's our position, to drop all other business of an enforcement and investigatory nature in order to serve that arrest warrant. All right. Then he -- it was getting on in the afternoon. He was busy with these other things. He might well have missed the petitioner, who arrived home at 4:15. He also testified that he had a desire not to embarrass the petitioner at his place of employment. Now, had the petitioner been released on bail, as he was perhaps the same afternoon, but very shortly after -- the record isn't completely clear. And had the case not gone to trial, the petitioner's employer would never have learned of the arrest. The petitioner would not be fired. There would not be any consequence. His wife would learn of it in any event.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: So once again we find the police are only interested in doing favors for the defendant.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: I would not state that as a general proposition and I would not be so naive to hold that that's the universal rule. However, I would submit that it is unparalleled, almost. It's impossible to find a police officer who could have given more attention to the observance of the defendant's constitutional rights than did this officer. He did not arrest --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: There were three officers.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: There were two officers from Santa Ana who accompanied him, and that was required --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Their was three.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Under California law because the place of arrest, petitioner's home, was outside the investigating officer's jurisdiction. And under California law, then, it has since been changed, Officer Del Coma would not have had the right to go outside the City of Orange to arrest petitioner at petitioner's Santa Ana home.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But regardless, there were three.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: There were three and there was --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Went to the man's home.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: They went to the man's home.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And waited for him.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: And waited 10 minutes for him?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And after he came home, they shook his house apart?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: They did not shake his house apart. They searched the house very carefully with petitioner's wife. They asked her to accompany them. They had her open drawers. They did not ransack.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: They were looking for the coins from this robbery.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Which they didn't find.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: No, that is not the case. The only -- and I wish to correct prior statement on that that was made in the preceding argument. The record shows that no coins were positively identified as coming from that burglary, but that is not to say that there were no coins seized. The inference is very strong that many of the coins were from the Money Vault burglary. We have set out the -- an inventory of the coins seized in our brief as an appendix, and if you compare those items to some of the items seized from Mr. Slocum's store during the burglary, there is a great similarity. But we know that coins are by their very nature almost fungible items, with a few exceptions, and it is very difficult to say, “That is my nickel.”

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr. George, put it this way, they knew exactly what they were looking for, coins.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And they seized items other than coins, which they did not know at that time were stolen.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: No, that is not the case. Mr. Monroe has implied --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: did they know about this other robbery before that?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: There's some indication that the arresting officer had some knowledge that there was another burglary, but he had no detailed information, as Mr. Monroe pointed out. There was not a wholesale --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Wouldn't he have needed that to make the search?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: No, he would not have needed that other information. He was looking for one thing only, and that was evidence, stolen fruits of the burglary for which he was arresting the petitioner. And that was --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: You suggest that everything he seized at that time was the kind of thing that might have been stolen from the Money Vault?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Exactly and I would like to clarify the record on that. There was some confusion. The item seized consisted of coins, coin paraphernalia, and one item that does not fall into that category -- a quantity of razor blades, and why razor blades? The officer was asked that. Well, because petitioner had said at the time of the arrest, “These razor blades are part of the coin transaction.” Now, that is susceptible of the inference that they could have been part of the Money Vault burglary transaction, but there is nothing that was not reasonably apparent as to be part of the Money Vault burglary that was seized. It was a very limited search that was confined by the objective of the officer, which was a permissible objective, namely, to return the stolen property. This was three-fourths of the man's inventory that had been wiped out, and the officers merely wanted to return that property to their rightful owner, and their search was 45 minutes and was confined to that. Now, petitioner has implied that this was a wholesale ransacking of the house. This isn't true. The record indicates unequivocally and we've set it out in detail in our brief that there were only two rooms that were really searched in the sense of what a search is, prying into hidden places. They were a bedroom, and a second bedroom --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: How large was the house?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: This was a 3-bedroom tract home, tract-size home. That's how it was described in the record, whatever that means, a suburban house. And these two rooms were searched in the sense of drawers being opened and petitioner's wife being asked to move the contents of the drawers to one side, to offer an innocuous explanation for any coins, and the officers left many coins. They only took those which appeared to be connected with the Money Vault. And now, these other rooms, the officer testified, he glanced into them. This was a cursory search. He looked into the garage. He looked into the child's room. But this was not a search. The search was confined to two rooms and its objective was perfectly permissible, and the search was of 45 minutes duration.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. George, may I ask you this. Is your desire to broaden out the right of search, does that indicate a desire on the part of law enforcement in California to wipe out the necessity of warrants, search warrants, warrants of every kind, in order to go in and search whenever they want to do so?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I'll tell you the reason I ask that. It's because in one case we had here a while back, it was stated to us that in the great City of Los Angeles, there were -- the Police Department got out, I think it was, fewer than 20 search warrants in an entire year.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That's incorrect information.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: How widespread is the practice of getting search warrants in Los Angeles?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Well, I will indicate that. I want to preface that by stating that the California Supreme Court, in its Keener decision has, which we have cited, has evidence a policy encouraging the use of warrants. We cite an article in the official publication of the California Peace Officers Bulletin encouraging officers to use search warrants, an article by the District Attorney.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, how prevalent are they, let's say, in the City of Los Angeles?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: The figures that we have for the Central District of Los Angeles, and some of the neighboring judicial districts -- this is not the whole county -- is 225 search warrants issued last year.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But that includes territory outside of Los Angeles?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Out of the city, but certain parts; yes. It's not countywide.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Because the Los Angeles Police have nothing to do with those?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes. How many in the City of Los Angeles?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: The City of Los Angeles, I would say approximately 200, I would think. Most of those in the Los Angeles Judicial District are in the City of Los Angeles.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Most of them, you say.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes. Now, this is a thirteen-fold increase since 1954, which is the year that the California Supreme Court anticipated this Court's decision in Mapp and required the exclusionary rule to be followed in California. And as Mr. Justice Harlan pointed out in his dissent to the Chapman case, California has been in the forefront of all the states in laying down rules protecting the rights of criminal suspects both in its -- in the field of confessions and search and seizure. The state has encouraged the use of warrants and they are being used increasingly. Each year the number increases. So -- and this officer here testified, he will obtain an arrest warrant, as he tried to do here. The law was changed after he obtained it. It was his policy to get one.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What did they get in the house?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: They got coins, coin paraphernalia, and this one item of a few razor blades.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Was that identified by anybody?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: As belonging to the place that had been burglarized?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: There were two burglaries, of course. And it was -- some of the coins were definitely identified as coming from a second burglary because they were unusual coins. They were odd metals. We've set them forth. Things like Alaska centennial coin or something like this, that the Pulati's recognized immediately, or else a container of coins with Mr. Pulati's handwriting crossed out. So, of course, those were able to be identified but they were 35 coins out of this quantity, a large quantity, of coins. The other coins, there was every strong inference that they were one and the same from the burglary for which the officers had made the arrest.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did the defendant testify?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: The defendant did testify. He gave an explanation that was so inherently improbable of his guilt that we would submit that it afforded consciousness of guilt evidence of his guilt. It was a highly unlikely explanation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What did you say? He testified?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: He testified in his own behalf and gave such a contrived explanation of how he had come in possession of some of these coins --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did he admit he had come into possession of them?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes, he did. And he said that he had purchased them from a man, and he didn't know whom, and he didn't collect medals but he had bought these medals anyway, and it was a very obvious attempt to fabricate an explanation for them. And in fact, he changed his testimony with reference to one coin, that he didn't know how he had obtained that, but later on he suddenly remembered that he had bought it. He gave no satisfactory explanation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Then the testimony that he was in possession of them is without dispute?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That is true, yes. In fact, there was a stipulation between Mr. Monroe and the District Attorney who tried the case that the coins identified by the Pulati's, found on the premises, were the same coins that were at the Pulati's home on the night of their burglary. That is in the record.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. George, was he tried for both burglaries?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: He was, and was convicted of both.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: He was convicted of both.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes, he was.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Although there was no identification of the coins from the second or the burglary on which they had had probable cause?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Well, no. There was no -- there were no coins found at petitioner's house that were tied into that burglary positively, but there was another coin, a very unusual coin with four faulty markings that two months, I believe, after the search, petitioner sold to a coin dealer, and this was a coin from the Money Vault burglary. And there's a great length of testimony in that regard and that was established clearly. It was a very unusual coin.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: This was a single trial for both burglaries?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes, it was. Both accounts were joined. I would like to address myself to the reasonableness of the search in light of all of the officer's conduct here. The officer had the admissions of the defendant to various civilian witnesses that he had committed the burglary in question. The defendant was negotiating to return the coins. The officer attempted to obtain an arrest warrant, conducted a great many sessions with the District Attorney's office, before he would deprive petitioner of his freedom. The District Attorney's office said, “Well, we think you have enough information, but go out and get another one, talk to this witness.” He did that. Only then that did he decide to make the arrest. The search was incident to the arrest. Clearly, it was the same time and place. The objective was as legitimate as any imaginable, to return thousands of dollars of stolen coins to their true owner. Only coins and coin paraphernalia that appeared to be connected with that particular burglary were seized. The manner of conducting the search was inherently reasonable. He had to bring along Santa Ana officers to legitimatize the search, since it was outside of his jurisdiction. He announced his entry, identified himself, asked whether he could enter, treated the defendant with utmost courtesy. He informed the defendant of the warrant, showed it to him, let him read it. He asked whether he could search, although consent was not given. But he informed the defendant that he had the right search anyway. The defendant's wife was the one who opened the drawer so there would not be any ransacking. The officer proceeded as quickly as he could. He took only 45 minutes to search. And then the officer took the defendant to the station and the two of them compiled an inventory of all of the seized property, defendant was shown the inventory and he signed a copy. Now, what can be unreasonable in this search? Certainly, not the scope. The scope was certainly proper. The objective of returning the coins to their stolen -- owner -- to the owner rather -- the returning the stolen coins to the owner would not be served by arbitrarily saying, “Well, you can search this room, but not that one, this cupboard, not that one.” If we started setting down standards like that, the police and the Courts would certainly be confused. What size of a room could you search? What circumference around the defendant? We submit that the Rabinowitz standard of reasonableness under all the circumstances is the proper standard especially in light of the fact that we have a state conviction here, under our federal system, and under the Court's decision in Ker, the States may develop workable rules of search and seizure.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But why --

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That's what California has done.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: What's your submission as to why an officer, when he arrests someone in his house, should be able to search any more broadly than the person of the defendant, to search for weapons or something like that, to guarantee the officer's own safety, or to just seize what is in plain sight? What's your basis for saying that he should be able to search for the fruits of a crime?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Well, our legal basis is, first of all, the Agnello decision. The full citation is in our brief at page 392. It indicates the evidence of -- that fruit of the crime may be searched. The Rabinowitz decision --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Anywhere in the house?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: It doesn't speak in terms of arbitrary geographical limitations.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, I know, but what's your submission then? You should be able to search for the fruits of the crime anywhere in the locality where you arrest the defendant?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Depending on the facts of this case. Let's say we have a television set involved here.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, why should you be able to search for the fruits of the crime, though, without a warrant?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Because the objective of returning them to their owner is certainly legitimate --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, why couldn't you get a warrant and do that?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: All right. In this case, we submit --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: If you know what the fruits of the crime are, why don't you come armed with a warrant for a search?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: It's easy to speak in terms of one case and say “Well, the officer could have taken the two or three hours to obtain a search warrant,” and that's what a commentator states it takes under ideal conditions to obtain one. But if you multiply this across the country, the last year for which there were statistics, there were 1,600,000 burglaries committed in the United States alone. Now, if you multiply that paper work --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I know, but if he had gotten a search warrant here, the search warrant would have specified only the fruits of the first robbery, isn't that right?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That's what --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Because that's all this policeman had any cause to suspect was connected with Chimel, isn't that right?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That's true, but all he was trying to --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Now by not getting a search warrant -- just a moment -- by not getting a search warrant, on your submission the police officer was able to make a broader search than he would have been able to make if he had gotten a search warrant. Am I right or wrong about that?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: I would say we do not agree with that, and I'll tell you why. The officer thought that he was only seizing coins from that burglary. He had -- there were many boxes and containers of coins. He would have delayed the defendant hours had it taken each box, sorted out a few medals that apparently that would not have been involved with one burglary, and gone through. The defendant would have been standing there all day. It was, in fact if he saw a box that appeared to contain coins from the Money Vault burglary, it's our position he was entitled to take that container, that box. The inventory of the items seized indicates that there were many, many -- there were perhaps hundreds or thousands of coins seized. There were many, many containers. The officer could not pick through each one individually. That would have been an impossible task.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But if he was searching for, or if he had had a warrant for certain coins, he might have been able to search anywhere in the house for those coins?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And if he encountered some other coins that he recognized as having been stolen from somewhere else, he could have seized them anyway, couldn't he?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: He could.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Some other cases?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes and even if not --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: You still haven't really gotten around to saying why you think that an officer should be able to search a house without a warrant for the fruits of a crime and you said that it would be a lot of trouble. So far you said that much.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes. Now, we recognize --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Is that it?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: No, that is not all. I don't think you can say it would just be trouble. You have to, under the Fourth Amendment, we submit, look at it in terms of what is reasonable and as part of reasonableness, this Court has always looked at the practical effect on law enforcement. Now, --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr. George, did you have probable cause to get a search warrant?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Very simple question, when the officer went to get the arrest warrant, why didn't he also ask for a search warrant because you have already admitted that one of his purposes was to retrieve this money?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, why didn't he write that other piece of paper?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Exactly. It's so simple that one wonders why not with an officer who is as eager to protect the defendant's constitutional rights. And that answer is, under this Court's decisions, specifically Marron and Stanford versus Texas, there is a very stringent requirement of particular description. It would not have been enough for the officer to say, to describe the property to be seized as coins and coin paraphernalia stolen from the Money Vault.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: I thought you said you had enough to get one.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes, but what would the officer have had to do to get one? He would have had to describe these coins in great detail.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: But what you're saying then is the officer has more latitude and lawfully has more latitude if he does not get a warrant than if he does get a warrant.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: No, we're not, because the officer could have obtained a search warrant. It might have taken him two weeks to do it because he would have had to distinguish the stolen coins from the coins that would be found on the premises. He knew that petitioner was a coin collector.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But realistically, though, do you think officers under our cases would get a search warrant when our cases aren't here to hold that if you make a valid arrest on probable cause, you may search incident to an arrest? Officers don't ordinarily come armed with -- let's assume for the moment that it would have been very easy, very easy, to get the search warrant, automatic, five minutes.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Officers often don't ordinarily come armed with both search and arrest warrant, do they?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: They do sometimes, and the reason --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I said ordinarily.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: It varies according to jurisdiction. I wouldn't say ordinarily across the country, no. But I think that there are incentives to obtain a search warrant that will make officers get them at time, and I would like to mention some of those to the Court. There is a presumption of legality that attends a search effected by a search warrant. In many jurisdictions and in California, if the officer does not have a search warrant, the search is presumed to be illegal in California and the burden is on the prosecution to obtain a search warrant, a justification for the search. It must show that there was probable cause and that the search was incident to an arrest. Secondly, the fact that there was a search warrant will insulate the officer from civil and criminal liability that will not be the case if there is not a search warrant. Finally, or rather additionally, there is also an opportunity for a broader search if there is a search warrant. The officer will be able to search outbuildings and throughout the premises and take as long as he wants if he has a search warrant. There will also be the possibility of search in the absence of the suspect. If he doesn't arrest the suspect at the house, of course, he couldn't search incident to an arrest. So this way, with a search warrant, he can search the house in the absence of the defendant. If there are multiple suspects, as there often are in narcotics raids or other conspiracy crimes, let's say, he can search many places. The police force can simultaneously, even if one or more defendants are absent.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, without the arrest, this search could not have been made. Do you agree with that?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Without a warrant?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That is without the arrest.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: What difference, then, would the state suggest it makes in terms of the permissibility of this search? What difference does it make that the arrest took place? I know you can say, “Well, the cases hold that,” but those cases are under attack here.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I just wonder why you think the arrest, just the fact of arresting a man in his house, gives the officers the right to make a search of the bedroom that otherwise they would not have had in the absence of arrest?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Because the officer had sufficient information to make the most basic invasion of the defendant's privacy that one can imagine. The seizure of his body. Now, how much more of an invasion of privacy is it for the officer to also seize items around the defendant?

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, I know, but if you had arrested him down at his office and made that basic invasion of privacy, it will be very difficult to go and search the house without a warrant.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: True, but you could have searched his office.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, I know, but you couldn't go and search his house.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes, but there's nothing to indicate that it was maneuvered.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: What difference does it make whether you arrest him in his house or in his office as to whether or not you can search his house without a warrant?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Well, there are various justifications. He might have weapons there. He might try to destroy evidence.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But they can say that they have a right to search for the fruits of a crime.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Anywhere in the house that fruits of that sort might be.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Of that sort.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: That is your submission.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes. It's different from a -- for a television theft or for stolen checks.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Drawer for a television set.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But you think they could have looked anywhere in the house that coins might have been hidden.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes, where coins could have been hidden and, of course, it's not open to --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I'm surprised that you don't suggest that once you arrest a man in his house, confederates, family, and everyone else is alerted -- are alerted and that it might be essential, if the officers have probable cause to make the arrest, and probable cause to believe that he has the fruits, or that they are somewhere, that there is some kind of urgency about looking for them, for the fruits, before they are made off with.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Well, of course, that would support the officer's not having to leave and come back with a warrant later.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But it doesn't get to why they didn't a warrant in the first place, does it?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Well, what it does is, the practical burden that would've been imposed on this officer to describe thousands of coins with sufficient particularity, as would be required under this Court's decision, to distinguish them from the defendant's coins --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well what decision do you say we said that?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Marron.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Did we go that far?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Marron describes the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Your submission is to get a warrant, it would have been necessary to specify every coin, hundreds of coins?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: At least to the degree necessary to distinguish them from the defendant's own coins, which the officers knew would be on the premises and because there is authority, and we have cited it in our brief, that the description must be adequate to segregate lawful property, the defendant's own property, from the items to be seized. And that is why the officer did not get a search warrant. Very frankly, off the record, I asked the officer, “Why didn't you,” as Justice Marshall asked, “If you were down there and you executed this affidavit for the arrest warrant, why didn't you also get a search warrant?” And that's it. He didn't want to get a search warrant that would get knocked out if it just described the coins in a general way.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, Mr. George, my problem is, I assume that the officer knew he was looking for.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: He did.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And when he did the search, he was looking for these coins.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And the only problem that you have and the officer has, is to writing down what he already has in his mind.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: He had -- he would have had to write down the description that the victim told him, “Well, this is my coin because it's this age, and maybe it's marked up this way, and therefore I know this nickel from somebody else's nickel.”

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, he had that information. It was just a question of putting it on paper.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That would have been quite a difficult problem and the officer chose to have the coins --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You mean difficult physically to dictate it or write it?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Well, both, and also to reduce certain conceptual matters to writing.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Could it also be the possibility that once he puts it down on paper, he would be restricted to that and he wouldn't be able to pick up something else?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Would that be a problem?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: That didn't trouble him, because he left coins on the premises. He left many, many coins.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: So there would be reason why he shouldn't have gotten a search warrant, is there?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Oh, there definitely is. It would have been an impossible task for him to do it, almost.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Impossible for him to write it?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Considering the number of coins. They are listed in our appendix here. There are hundreds, if not thousands, of coins until I get --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And he couldn't write it?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: This would've been an unreasonable burden --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Oh, it is an unreasonable burden for a police officer to write.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: In certain situations to give this kind of a description --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: I understand your position.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Would be like describing the grains of sand on a beach.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, Mr. George, if they couldn't differentiate at the police station, or at the Court when they're getting a warrant of arrest, what coins they're interested in, how did they differentiate when they were in the house as to what coins they would take and what ones the would leave.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: The officer apparently had a general description, at least, of particular types of coins right with him, and if he saw a particular container --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Couldn't he have given that to the judge?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Couldn't he have given that to the judge?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: It might not have been sufficient. Of course, this Court has been very stringent. If you don't dot your “i's” and cross your “t's” in getting the search warrant --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: So because he didn't think that was sufficient information that he had, he thought he couldn't get a search warrant, so he thought he would do it without one.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: He thought it was sufficient information. He thought that it would have been very, very difficult to meet this burden of setting it all out in writing and having it stand up and he thought it wasn't necessary in view of this Court's decisions stretching back many, many years, that a search is reasonable if it is incident to an arrest. That is why he thought that he did not have to.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. George, may I ask you this one. When you and I were discussing the number of search warrants, I thought you indicated that at some place in the record here there were some figures on that.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Not in the record. No. The only figure related to that is the number of burglary investigations that Officer Del Coma himself had for a month, but the number of search warrants --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But you told me something like 200 in the Central District of Los Angeles and so forth. You don't have any figures like that in?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: I have only a letter from the District Attorney's office. I thought the Court might be interested in that point and I inquired.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Would there be any objection, counsel, to it?

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: None I also have some figures.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Very well we -- I'd like to have them. Just might not be relevant, but it would be interesting anyway.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: May I file with the Court the letter that I received from the District Attorney's office?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes, please.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Mr. George, I wanted to ask you one question. You might not have time to do it between now and 12:00. It's about a minute and a half. You said many times that they had probable cause to believe he was guilty and that justified an arrest?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Could you state briefly what evidence there was that they had?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes. The defendant, prior to the burglary itself, had approached the victim and said, “How would you like to have you place knocked off,” and “Do you carry insurance,” and all of that. Shortly before the burglary, just a few hours, he told a friend, “I have a big job going, we're going to knock off a coin shop, maybe $50,000 or $60,000 are involved.”

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Was this a pawnshop?

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: A coin shop.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: A coin shop.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Then shortly afterwards, he told the same friend, “Well, we broke through the two walls and we came across the wires, but we didn't really go through with the job completely.” Then the next day he told the fellow, “Oh, well I was just kidding ,don't believe what I said.” Then he told another person after the burglary, “I am involved in the Money Vault burglary, I may be going to jail on that.” Then he negotiated with the police for the return of the coins. This man broadcast all over town that he --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: He negotiated for the return of the coins.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: For the return of the coins, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What -- how do you mean “negotiate”.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: He and another suspect and an attorney, who the record indicates was representing both suspects, negotiated for the return of the coins for a civil release.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Actually negotiated at the police station.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: At the police station so this --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Before arrest.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Guilt and the probable cause was beyond reasonable doubt. I can't imagine a case with more.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well He did claim, though, that he had bought these things, did he not? He didn't admit to any burglarizing.

turns.name: KEITH C. MONROE
turns.text: No, he did not admit.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: He claimed that he had bought these from somebody, fictitious or otherwise.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: He gave a very unlikely explanation.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Very well. We we'll recess now.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: For one minute to --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes, you may have one minute.

turns.name: RONALD M. GEORGE
turns.text: Thank you very much.
